proofs.proof_tex:
Put \(R_n=(P_0^\circ)^n\), and let \(L_{\theta,n}=dP_{\theta,n}/dP_0^\circ\) denote the one-observation likelihood ratio. Under the base law, conditional on \((X,A)\), the observed outcome is uniform on \(\{-B,B\}\). Under \(P_{\theta,n}\), its conditional mean is \((2A-1)\Delta_{\theta,n}(X)/2\). Therefore
\[
 L_{\theta,n}(O)=1+\frac{(2A-1)Y\Delta_{\theta,n}(X)}{2B^2}.
\]
Let
\[
 J_2=\int_{[0,1]^d}\psi(z)^2\,dz
 \quad\text{and}\quad
 q_n=\frac{a_n^2h_n^dJ_2}{4B^2}.
\]
For two sign vectors \(\theta\) and \(\theta'\), independence and symmetry under \(P_0^\circ\), followed by disjointness of the cell supports, give
\[
 \begin{aligned}
 E_{P_0^\circ}[L_{\theta,n}L_{\theta',n}]
 &=1+\frac{1}{4B^2}\int_{\mathcal X}\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)\Delta_{\theta',n}(x)\,dx\\
 &=1+q_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}\theta_\ell\theta'_\ell.
 \end{aligned}
\]
Taking products over the \(n\) independent observations and then averaging over the two sign vectors yields
\[
 1+\chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)
 =2^{-2N_n}\sum_{\theta,\theta'}
 \left(1+q_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}\theta_\ell\theta'_\ell\right)^n.
\]
If \(R_1,\ldots,R_{N_n}\) are independent Rademacher variables, the last display equals
\[
 E\left(1+q_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}R_\ell\right)^n.
\]
The factor inside the power is nonnegative because it is an integral of a product of nonnegative likelihood ratios. The elementary inequalities \(1+x\le e^x\) for \(x\ge-1\) and \(\cosh(t)\le e^{t^2/2}\) therefore imply
\[
 \begin{aligned}
 1+\chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)
 &\le E\exp\left(nq_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}R_\ell\right)\\
 &=\{\cosh(nq_n)\}^{N_n}\\
 &\le\exp\left(\frac{N_nn^2q_n^2}{2}\right)\\
 &=\exp\left\{K n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}\right\},
 \end{aligned}
\]
where one may take \(K=J_2^2/(32B^4)\).

We next check that the testing calibration works for every admissible pair, without imposing \(3s_1\alpha<d\). Since \(N_n\le c_Nh_n^{s_1\alpha-d}\), \(a_n=c_ah_n^{s_1}\), \(D=d+s_1\alpha+4s_1\), and \(h_n=c_hn^{-2/D}\),
\[
 n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}
 \le c_Nc_a^4n^2h_n^{d+s_1\alpha+4s_1}
 =c_Nc_a^4c_h^D.
\]
Fix any \(\varepsilon\in(0,1-2\gamma)\). First choose \(c_a>0\) small enough for the Hölder and mean-envelope requirements in \(\mathrm{lem:sparse-family-membership}\); next choose \(c_N>0\) small enough for its margin requirement; finally choose \(c_h>0\) small enough that
\[
 Kc_Nc_a^4c_h^D\le\log(1+\varepsilon^2).
\]
These choices are fixed in \(n\) and satisfy all of the membership and testing restrictions simultaneously. Moreover, \(s_1\alpha<1\le d\), so \(N_nh_n^d\asymp h_n^{s_1\alpha}\to0\); hence the required \(N_n\) disjoint cells fit inside \([1/4,3/4]^d\) for all sufficiently large \(n\). We conclude that
\[
 \chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)
 \le \exp\{K n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}\}-1
 \le\varepsilon^2
\]
eventually for every admissible pair.

By the declared type of the base law and \(\mathrm{def:base-law}\), \(P_0^\circ\in\mathcal P_0\subset\mathcal U\) and \(V(P_0^\circ)=0\). By \(\mathrm{lem:sparse-family-membership}\), every component of \(\overline Q_n\) belongs to \(\mathcal P_1\subset\mathcal U\) and has value \(\delta_n\). Fix an arbitrary \((C_n)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\). The definition of uniform asymptotic honesty supplies a deterministic sequence \(r_n\downarrow0\) such that, eventually,
\[
 R_n\{0\in C_n\}\ge1-\gamma-r_n,
 \qquad
 \overline Q_n\{\delta_n\in C_n\}\ge1-\gamma-r_n.
\]
For every event \(E\), Cauchy--Schwarz applied to \(d\overline Q_n/dR_n-1\) gives
\[
 |\overline Q_n(E)-R_n(E)|
 \le\frac12\{\chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)\}^{1/2}
 \le\frac{\varepsilon}{2}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
 R_n\{0\in C_n,\ \delta_n\in C_n\}
 \ge1-2\gamma-2r_n-\frac{\varepsilon}{2}.
\]
Because \(C_n\) is connected, this event implies \(\operatorname{length}(C_n)\ge\delta_n\). Thus
\[
 \liminf_{n\to\infty}
 \frac{E_{R_n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]}{\delta_n}
 \ge1-2\gamma-\frac{\varepsilon}{2}
 =:c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}>0.
\]
This proves the chi-square and expected-length assertions for every admissible pair.

For the final conditional comparison, \(\mathrm{lem:sparse-family-membership}\) gives \(\delta_n\asymp n^{-\xi}\), and direct algebra gives
\[
 \xi-\frac12
 =\frac{3s_1\alpha-d}{2(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)}.
\]
If \(3s_1\alpha<d\), then \(\xi<1/2\) and \(\delta_n/n^{-1/2}\to\infty\) up to fixed positive multiplicative constants. The just-proved union-honest lower bound is then strictly slower than root \(n\) at \(P_0^\circ\), while \(\mathrm{prop:oracle-tie-rootn}\) gives an \(O(n^{-1/2})\)-length interval when coverage is required only over the exact-tie stratum.

Fix an arbitrary honest interval sequence \((C_n)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\), and write
\[
 e_n=E_{(P_0^\circ)^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)].
\]
By \(\mathrm{lem:parametric-tie-length-lower-bound}\), there is \(c_\gamma>0\) such that
\[
 \liminf_{n\to\infty}\frac{e_n}{n^{-1/2}}\ge c_\gamma.
\]
By \(\mathrm{thm:sparse-near-tie-adaptation-lower-bound}\), for the fixed admissible calibration and some \(c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}>0\),
\[
 \liminf_{n\to\infty}\frac{e_n}{\delta_n}\ge c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}.
\]
Consequently, for all sufficiently large \(n\),
\[
 e_n\ge\frac{c_\gamma}{2}n^{-1/2},
 \qquad
 e_n\ge\frac{c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}}{2}\delta_n,
\]
and hence
\[
 e_n\ge c_*\{n^{-1/2}\vee\delta_n\},
 \qquad
 c_*:=\frac12\min\{c_\gamma,c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}\}>0.
\]
The base law belongs to both \(\mathcal P_0\) and \(\mathcal P_1\) by \(\mathrm{lem:parametric-tie-length-lower-bound}\). Therefore, for each \(j\in\{0,1\}\),
\[
 \limsup_{n\to\infty}\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_j}
 \frac{E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]}{\ell_n}
 \ge c_*.
\]
Taking the infimum over all honest sequences proves \(\mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(\ell)\ge c_*\).

The upper assertion follows from the single interval sequence constructed in \(\mathrm{thm:split-sample-union-upper-bracket}\): for a finite \(K_*\), it satisfies
\[
 \limsup_{n\to\infty}\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_j}
 \frac{E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n^\dagger)]}{u_n}
 \le K_*,
 \qquad j\in\{0,1\}.
\]
Inserting it into each defining infimum gives \(\mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(u)\le K_*\).

By \(\mathrm{lem:sparse-family-membership}\), \(\delta_n\asymp n^{-\xi}\), so
\[
 \ell_n\asymp n^{-\min\{1/2,\xi\}},
 \qquad
 u_n\asymp n^{-\min\{1/2,\rho\}}.
\]
The admissibility condition \(s_1\alpha<1\le d\) implies \(d-s_1\alpha>0\), and direct subtraction gives
\[
 \xi-\rho
 =\frac{s_1(\alpha+1)(d-s_1\alpha)}{(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)(d+2s_1)}>0.
\]
Also,
\[
 \rho-\frac12=\frac{2s_1\alpha-d}{2(2s_1+d)},
 \qquad
 \xi-\frac12=\frac{3s_1\alpha-d}{2(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)}.
\]
If \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\), then \(\rho\ge1/2\) and \(\xi>\rho\), so both scales are root \(n\). If \(2s_1\alpha<d\le3s_1\alpha\), then \(\rho<1/2\le\xi\), giving the second regime. If \(3s_1\alpha<d\), then \(\rho<\xi<1/2\), giving the third regime.

proposed_statement_changes.current: Does the union-calibrated route in \(\mathrm{def:union-upper-route}\) yield a sequence \((C_n^\dagger)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) with \(\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_0}E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n^\dagger)]=O(\delta_n)\) and the corresponding near-tie risk controlled on \(\mathcal P_1\)?
proposed_statement_changes.proposed: Does the union-calibrated route in \(\mathrm{def:union-upper-route}\) yield a sequence \((C_n^{\mathrm{ms}})_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) with \(\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_0}E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}})]=O(\delta_n)\) and the corresponding near-tie risk controlled on \(\mathcal P_1\)?
proposed_statement_changes.reason: The current notation collides with the proved split-sample interval and permits the open question to be read as referring to that fixed construction. The proposed text restricts the hypothetical multiscale route to its own symbol \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\), while leaving the substantive rate question unresolved.

proposed.statement: For every admissible pair, there are fixed admissible choices of \(c_h,c_a,c_N\) and \(\varepsilon\in(0,1-2\gamma)\) such that \(\chi^2(\overline Q_n,(P_0^\circ)^n)\le\exp\{K n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}\}-1\le\varepsilon^2\) eventually. Hence every \((C_n)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) obeys \(\liminf_{n\to\infty}E_{(P_0^\circ)^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]/\delta_n\ge c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}>0\). If, in addition, \(3s_1\alpha<d\), then \(\xi<1/2\), so union honesty forces a strictly slower-than-root-\(n\) expected length at an exact-tie law even though \(\mathcal P_0\) alone admits \(O(n^{-1/2})\) length.
proposed.justification: The sparse-mixture chi-square and expected-length conclusions hold for every admissible pair; \(3s_1\alpha<d\) is reserved solely for the strict slower-than-root-\(n\) comparison.
proposed.gap: The explicit causal mixture establishes one component of the lower frontier but does not by itself include the parametric root-\(n\) obstruction.
proposed.consumer: Together with the parametric tie lower bound, it determines the unavoidable scale faced by a union-honest clinical interval at the exact-tie law.

proposed_core_edits.reason: The iid product likelihood-ratio and chi-square calculation explicitly uses \(\mathrm{ass:iid-sampling}\), so the theorem's dependency list must declare that assumption.

proposed_core_edits.name: C_n^\dagger

proposed.name: C_n^\dagger
proposed.def: The explicit split-sample connected interval sequence constructed in \(\mathrm{thm:split-sample-union-upper-bracket}\).

proposed_core_edits.reason: The symbol \(C_n^\dagger\) is used by the proved split-sample upper theorem and must no longer denote the unresolved multiscale construction.

proposed_core_edits.name: C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}

proposed.name: C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}
proposed.def: A hypothetical connected interval sequence associated with the unresolved multiscale union-calibrated route in \(\mathrm{def:union-upper-route}\).

proposed_core_edits.reason: A distinct symbol is required for the unresolved multiscale interval so that it cannot be confused with the proved split-sample interval \(C_n^\dagger\).
Rebuild every assumption's direct \(\mathrm{used\_by}\) list after adding \(\mathrm{ass:iid-sampling}\) to the sparse lower theorem.

open_obligations.what_is_open: Whether the hypothetical multiscale sequence \((C_n^{\mathrm{ms}})_n\) can be uniformly honest over \(\mathcal U\) while attaining expected length \(O(\delta_n)\) on \(\mathcal P_0\) and controlling the near-tie risk on \(\mathcal P_1\).
open_obligations.obstruction: The frozen multiscale route is only a construction handle: it does not specify an observable bias certificate or prove simultaneous coverage. Moreover, the parametric tie lower bound rules out \(O(\delta_n)\) whenever \(3s_1\alpha>d\), because then \(\delta_n=o(n^{-1/2})\).
open_obligations.attempted: The proved split-sample learned-policy interval was used as the explicit fallback. It supplies honest coverage through independent policy evaluation and a Markov-calibrated regret allowance, but its length has order \(n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\), not generally \(\delta_n\).
open_obligations.partial_result: The explicit sequence \((C_n^\dagger)_n\) is finite-sample honest over \(\mathcal U\) and has uniform expected length \(O(n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho})\) on both strata. Attainment of \(O(\delta_n)\) is impossible when \(3s_1\alpha>d\) and remains open in the nonexcluded regimes.

open_obligations.what_is_open: Whether the lower scale \(n^{-1/2}\vee n^{-\xi}\) is attainable throughout \(2s_1\alpha<d\), or whether a different least-favorable causal experiment raises the lower scale to the current achievable order \(n^{-\rho}\).
open_obligations.obstruction: The sparse randomized-sign experiment yields the exponent \(\xi\), while the explicit split-sample construction yields the smaller exponent \(\rho\). The current core contains neither a sharper honest upper construction nor a stronger indistinguishability experiment that closes this exponent gap.
open_obligations.attempted: The sparse-mixture and parametric local experiments were combined with the split-sample learned-policy interval. This closes the root-\(n\) subregime but leaves the two nonparametric regimes separated.
open_obligations.partial_result: For both strata, the minimax expected-length coefficient is bounded below at \(\ell_n\asymp n^{-\min\{1/2,\xi\}}\) and bounded above at \(u_n\asymp n^{-\min\{1/2,\rho\}}\). The scales match at root \(n\) when \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\); the regimes \(2s_1\alpha<d\le3s_1\alpha\) and \(3s_1\alpha<d\) remain open.

prose_updates.tldr: Under iid sampling, known one-half randomization, bounded outcomes, Hölder outcome regressions, and a margin condition that permits a tie atom, a sparse randomized-sign experiment forces every union-honest connected interval for the optimal policy value to have expected length at least \(n^{-1/2}\vee n^{-\xi}\) at the exact-tie base law, where \(\xi=2s_1(\alpha+1)/(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)\). The explicit split-sample learned-policy interval \(C_n^\dagger\) is uniformly honest and has length \(O(n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho})\), where \(\rho=s_1(\alpha+1)/(2s_1+d)\). These scales coincide at root \(n\) when \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\); outside that subregime, attainment of the sparse lower scale by the distinct hypothetical multiscale interval \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) remains open.

project_justification.gap: Existing inference for exceptional optimal-treatment laws does not provide this two-stratum expected-length adaptation diagram for a connected interval that is honest simultaneously over exact ties and Hölder near ties with an allowed tie atom. The unresolved issue is sharply localized: when \(2s_1\alpha<d\), the proved lower exponent \(\min\{1/2,\xi\}\) and achievable exponent \(\min\{1/2,\rho\}\) differ.
project_justification.niche: The note studies honest adaptation for the causal optimal-policy value, taking its randomized-trial identifying functional as given. Its distinctive ingredients are an explicit dominated sparse causal mixture for the lower bound and a single data-driven split-sample learned-policy interval for the upper bound.
project_justification.fill: The lower calculation holds for every admissible pair; the inequality \(3s_1\alpha<d\) is needed only for the corollary that the sparse scale is strictly slower than root \(n\). Combining that calculation with a parametric tie submodel gives the unified lower scale \(n^{-1/2}\vee n^{-\xi}\) on both strata. The proved split-sample interval \(C_n^\dagger\) supplies the honest upper scale \(n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\); the symbol \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) is reserved for the unresolved multiscale route.

prose_updates.related_work: Luedtke--van der Laan and subsequent work analyze inference at exceptional laws, while policy-learning results supply regret control under smoothness and margin conditions. Cai--Low-style testing arguments explain how indistinguishable alternatives constrain expected interval length, and Armstrong--Kolesar emphasize the cost of honest bias control under predeclared restrictions. Ponomarev--Semenova and Liu--Liu--Sasaki--Wan address related non-uniqueness-robust or representative-policy inference problems. None of those results supplies the present explicit sparse randomized-sign causal product experiment together with this exact-tie/near-tie union bracket. The current result is a proved lower/upper bracket, not a claim of a globally matched frontier.
prose_updates.interpretation: At an exact biomarker-response tie, an analysis restricted to the tie stratum can use a root-\(n\) interval, but a procedure that must also cover nearby heterogeneous treatment effects can be forced to be wider. For individualized rilpivirine-versus-efavirenz regime analysis, the result separates three facts: the unavoidable lower cost, the usable uniformly honest split-sample interval \(C_n^\dagger\), and the remaining mathematical gap represented by the hypothetical multiscale interval \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\). The proved interval evaluates a policy learned on an independent training half and adds an explicit allowance for its worst-case regret.
prose_updates.technical_internal_limitation: The split-sample construction converts an expected local-polynomial policy-regret bound into coverage by Markov calibration. This produces the exponent \(\rho=s_1(\alpha+1)/(2s_1+d)\), which is strictly smaller than \(\xi\) under the admissibility condition. Thus \(C_n^\dagger\) attains the unified lower scale only in the root-\(n\) subregime \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\). When \(2s_1\alpha<d\), it is unknown whether a fully specified multiscale interval \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) with sharper high-probability regret or bias control improves the upper rate, or whether a different causal least-favorable experiment strengthens the lower bound.
prose_updates.honest_scope: The sampling model is iid observed-data sampling from a randomized binary-treatment law with known propensity \(1/2\), bounded potential outcomes, a conditional-variance floor, and a design density bounded above and below. The union contains an exact-tie Hölder stratum and a near-tie Hölder stratum satisfying the written margin inequality while allowing an arbitrary tie atom. The conclusions concern connected two-sided intervals for the identified optimal-policy value. They do not cover observational propensity estimation, unknown smoothness or margin parameters beyond this frozen pair, nonconnected confidence sets, or a globally sharp adaptation frontier in the open regimes.

statement_notes.justification: The sparse-mixture chi-square and expected-length conclusions hold for every admissible pair; \(3s_1\alpha<d\) is reserved solely for the strict slower-than-root-\(n\) comparison.
statement_notes.gap: The explicit causal mixture establishes one component of the lower frontier but does not by itself include the parametric root-\(n\) obstruction.
statement_notes.consumer: Together with the parametric tie lower bound, it determines the unavoidable scale faced by a union-honest clinical interval at the exact-tie law.

statement_notes.justification: The proved symbol \(C_n^\dagger\) denotes the split-sample interval. The separate symbol \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) denotes the hypothetical multiscale route whose sharper rate remains open.
statement_notes.gap: Attainment of \(O(\delta_n)\) by \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) remains open where it is not excluded by the parametric tie lower bound.
statement_notes.consumer: A sharper answer would determine whether multiscale calibration can improve on the current conservative split-sample clinical interval.

statement_notes.justification: The root-\(n\) subregime \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\) is settled by matching lower and upper scales, while the remaining regimes have an explicit honest bracket.
statement_notes.gap: For \(2s_1\alpha<d\), neither the split-sample upper construction nor the two current lower experiments closes the exponent gap.
statement_notes.consumer: The open regimes specify where a new interval construction or a new least-favorable causal family would change practical precision guarantees.

statement_notes.justification: The theorem combines the parametric tie experiment, the sparse randomized-sign experiment, and the explicit split-sample interval into one actionable two-sided rate bracket.
statement_notes.gap: The bracket is matched only when \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\); its tightness remains unknown in the two nonparametric regimes.
statement_notes.consumer: The bracket gives downstream analysts a uniformly honest interval scale now and identifies exactly where sharper theory could improve expected length.
